name: Teo Macero
description: American jazz saxophonist, composer, and record producer (1925–2008)
abstract: Attilio Joseph "Teo" Macero (October 30, 1925 – February 19, 2008) was an American jazz record producer, saxophonist, and composer. He was a producer at Columbia Records for twenty years.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Contemporary Jazz Composers Series
Kool Jazz Festival: Musicians for Monk

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Liebman
about_quotes.evidence: You’ve done over the years several dates for Teo Macero for . . . He would call you and say, “Be there tomorrow,” and the most disorganized of all time were his dates.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Liebman — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: Miles wanted to document the process as it was happening and so he got Teo [Macero, record producer] to put the tape on... It was a flowing, organic session that Teo later edited and spliced together the best parts
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Brubeck
about_quotes.evidence: Teo Macero and Miles invited me to the session at Columbia where they’d be editing that day. I was told they’d be working on The Duke. When I came into the control room, I was introduced to Gil.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Brubeck — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: I’m thinking, Miles Davis called me on the phone. He – “I want to make a record.” ... Teo Macero. I think he invited John. ... Teo finally asked him, when Miles came.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: Mingus and Teo and Teddy were the basis for this whole thing, like a workshop type of thing where the guys would bring in music and we would play it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: And then through Teo Macero, his producer, through his artistry of putting all the different tracks together to tell a story, he did that very well.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, April 7, 2025
